sections.heading: Classification
sections.content: Vulvar cancer is a malignant neoplasm arising from the vulvar complex, which includes the labia majora, labia minora, clitoris, mons pubis, perineum, and vulvar vestibule. The majority of vulvar cancers originate from the squamous epithelium of these structures. Cutaneous vulvar melanoma is defined as melanocytic lesions that occur on the vulva vestibule outside Hart's line, whereas mucosal vulvovaginal melanoma is defined as lesions that occur on the vulva vestibule inside Hart's line [MS-2, VM-1A]. Vulvar cancer accounts for 5% to 8% of all gynecologic malignancies [MS-1].

sections.heading: Anatomical Considerations
sections.content: The vulvar vestibule is bounded laterally by the labia minora, medially by the vaginal introitus, superiorly by the clitoral complex, and inferiorly by the perineal body. The vulvar skin is a specialized mucocutaneous transition zone with unique histologic features including hair-bearing (labia majora) and non-hair-bearing (labia minora) regions, with the vestibular mucosa transitioning to vaginal mucosa at the introitus. Anatomically, the lymphatic drainage of the vulva follows predictable patterns: lymphatics from the labia majora and lower labia minora drain primarily to the inguinofemoral lymph nodes, while structures above Hart's line (clitoris, urethral meatus) have bilateral drainage pathways. The perineal body and posterior labia may drain to the ipsilateral groin as well as to posterior thigh or ischiorectal lymph nodes. This lymphatic anatomy is clinically critical because inguinofemoral lymph node status is the single most important prognostic factor and determinant of treatment in vulvar cancer [MS-4, MS-5].

sections.heading: Staging
sections.content: Vulvar cancer staging follows the FIGO (International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics) staging system, which was revised in 2021 to allow incorporation of cross-sectional imaging findings alongside surgical-pathologic staging [ST-1].
